pmos

Landing Page Design & Copywriting — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A landing page is a single web page built to do one job — turn a visitor who just clicked an ad, email, or link into someone who takes one specific action. This primer maps the base layer every product manager should recognize: what a landing page is and how it differs from a homepage, the standard anatomy of a high-converting page, why you start with the value proposition (and the customer's jobs, pains, and gains behind it), message match and the discipline of one-page-one-job, how to write headlines and body copy using the customer's own words, what makes a call-to-action (CTA) button get clicked, how social proof and the psychology of persuasion build trust, how reducing friction matters as much as adding persuasion, and how teams use conversion-rate optimization (CRO) and A/B testing to improve pages over time. The named camps — conversion copywriters, optimization researchers, and usability writers — emphasize different levers, but converge on one idea: clarity beats cleverness, and the page exists to serve the visitor's goal, not the brand's ego. Written for any PM (product manager): every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the conversion-copywriting canon (Joanna Wiebe/Copyhackers, Harry Dry, Oli Gardner/Unbounce), the value-proposition and persuasion literature (Strategyzer, Cialdini, MECLABS), usability (Steve Krug), and CRO practitioners (CXL-adjacent sources, Crazy Egg, Kissmetrics). 17 verified sources.

What a landing page actually is

A landing page is, in Wikipedia's plain definition, "a single web page that appears in response to clicking on a search engine optimized search result, marketing promotion, marketing email or an online advertisement." The key word is single. Where a homepage is a lobby — a general entrance that has to serve every visitor and point in many directions — a landing page is a hallway built for one trip: someone arrived because of a specific promise, and the page exists to deliver on that promise and ask for one specific thing in return.

That one specific thing is called the conversion — the action you want the visitor to take, such as starting a free trial, booking a demo, or buying a product. Practitioners distinguish two broad shapes of landing page. A reference page surrounds the visitor "with all available information about the targeted product to convince them to take action," while a transactional page has a narrower purpose: "to persuade a visitor to take action by completing a transaction" — usually by filling in a form or clicking a buy button.

The discipline that follows from this definition is focus. Because a landing page serves one marketing action, anything that doesn't move the visitor toward that action is a distraction. Marketing teams routinely "remove the navigation menu and limit internal and external links on the page" for exactly this reason — every extra link is an invitation to leave. For a PM, the mental model is simple: a homepage is a map of your whole product; a landing page is a single, well-lit path to one door.

The anatomy of a landing page

Most high-converting landing pages share a recognizable skeleton. The guidance from conversion practitioners converges on a sequence that, in involve.me's words, is "a focused sequence that takes visitors from promise to proof to action": a hero section, the unique value proposition, benefits and features, social proof, the primary CTA or form, objection handlers, and a deliberately sparse footer. The order matters because it mirrors how a skeptical stranger actually decides — first "what is this?", then "why should I believe you?", then "what do I do?".

The hero is the top of the page, the part visible before any scrolling (often called above the fold, a phrase borrowed from newspapers that were displayed folded in half). It carries four elements: a clear, benefit-driven headline; a subheadline that adds context; one supporting visual; and a single call to action. Everything below the hero earns the right to be read by what the hero promised.

Below the hero, the benefits section connects each feature to a tangible outcome, social proof shows that other people trust you, objection handlers (frequently-asked-questions blocks, guarantees, security badges) answer the "yes, but…" worries, and the footer stays clean. The single most repeated rule across the canon is involve.me's blunt summary — "One page. One job." — backed by the striking claim that "removing navigation menus alone can double conversion rates by reducing distractions." Crazy Egg adds the design counterpart for the CTA itself: "ensure that users can see a CTA button as soon as they arrive on the page, without having to scroll."

Start with the value proposition

Before any word of copy, you need to know what you are actually offering and to whom. The value proposition is the core promise — the reason this product is worth a stranger's attention. The most widely taught tool for sharpening it is Strategyzer's Value Proposition Canvas, created by Alex Osterwalder, which forces you to map the customer before you describe the product.

The canvas has two halves. On the customer side sit three things: customer jobs — "the functional, social, and emotional tasks customers try to accomplish"; pains — "the negative experiences, risks, and obstacles customers face when trying to get their jobs done"; and gains — "the positive outcomes and benefits customers expect or desire." On the product side sit your products and services, your pain relievers, and your gain creators. The whole exercise aims at one target Strategyzer calls fit: "the point when your pain relievers and gain creators directly address the most important customer pains and gains." A landing page that converts is almost always one where this fit was found before the copywriting began.

There is a reason to lead with clarity rather than cleverness here. The MECLABS Institute, founded by Flint McGlaughlin, distilled a thinking tool it calls the Conversion Sequence Heuristic from testing "more than 10,000 sales paths over the past 20 years" — "a way of looking at a sales path and going through elements of the customer's experience of a particular page or process." The levers it weighs are the visitor's motivation and the force of the value proposition, working against the friction and anxiety in the process. The diagram below shows that balance: persuasion only matters once the value proposition is clear enough to tip the scale.

Further reading: amazon.com — Amazon listing for April Dunford's book 'Obviously Awesome,' covering product positioning strategy; no content available beyond the Amazon storefront.

Message match and the one-job rule

The fastest way to lose a hard-won click is to break the promise that earned it. Message match is, per Unbounce's glossary, "a measure of how well your landing page copy matches the phrasing of the ad or link that brought the visitor there." When the headline of the page echoes the words of the ad someone just clicked, it reassures them they are in the right place; when it doesn't, the page "essentially wastes the attention and trust visitors initially granted through their click."

How common is the failure? Oli Gardner of Unbounce examined 300 paid ads and concluded that the vast majority broke the connection between ad and page — which is why his talk is titled "98% of Marketers are Doing it WRONG!" The cousin of message match is what Gardner calls attention ratio: "the ratio of interaction points (links) to the number of calls to action on any given page." A typical homepage runs around 40:1 — thirty-nine things to click for every one you actually want clicked — whereas a focused landing page aims for 1:1.

You can see this discipline in real teardowns. In Unbounce's analysis of high-converting pages, the scheduling tool Later succeeded by keeping "conversion scent throughout the campaign. The offer matches what's in the ad, in the email" and on the page. For a PM, message match is the cheapest conversion win available: it costs nothing but coordination between whoever writes the ad and whoever writes the page.

Further reading: news.aakashg.com — Aakash Gupta's newsletter article on optimising how you consume and process information.

Writing headlines that work

The headline is the first thing a visitor reads and often the only thing — so it carries most of the page's persuasive weight. The conversion-copywriting view, championed by Joanna Wiebe of Copyhackers, is that there is no magic word count. As she puts it, "You'll need to provide sufficient copy to tap into the visitors' motivation for coming, and amplify that motivation so they take your desired action. If you provide too little copy, you may not adequately answer the visitors' questions; too much and you risk turning them off." The goal is a high word-to-meaning ratio — few words carrying a lot of value.

Where do the right words come from? Not from your imagination, the copywriters insist, but from the customer. This is voice-of-customer (VoC) research — writing with the customer's own language instead of invented marketing claims. Wiebe describes copy that taps "directly into what your customer's thinking and feeling," and recounts a page for the antiperspirant Sweatblock that "beat the control" — the existing version it was tested against — precisely because "all of the problems that follow, came directly from reviews for Sweatblock on Amazon."

The practical technique is review mining: reading the reviews of your own product, or competitors', or even unrelated products that solve the same pain, and harvesting the exact phrases people use. Wiebe even offers a search shortcut — `site:amazon.com inurl:"product-reviews" "tired of" [keyword]` — to surface real frustrations fast. The headline that results sounds like something the reader would say to a friend, which is exactly why it lands. Unbounce's teardowns reinforce the brevity instinct, too: the course platform edX won because "shorter copy worked better than longer copy."

Further reading: writingcooperative.com — Breakdown of the Amazon 6-pager narrative document format, explaining its structure, purpose, and why Amazon uses long-form memos instead of slide decks to drive strategic decision-making.

Persuasive body copy

If the headline earns attention, the body copy has to keep it — and the most useful test for whether body copy is doing its job comes from Harry Dry of Marketing Examples. He gives copy three questions to pass. First, visualizability: "Can I visualize it?" If a reader cannot picture what you are describing, it is probably empty. Second, falsifiability: "Can I prove it true or false?" — a specific, checkable claim like "get your clients from 'signup' to 'first payment' in under 24 hours" beats a vague boast every time.

Third, uniqueness: "Can nobody else write this?" A line that any competitor could paste onto their own site — Dry's example is "We deliver innovative solutions tailored for your needs" — says nothing. His verdict is memorable: "If you can't say yes to all three, you've probably written fluff." These three tests — concrete, provable, distinctive — are the practical complement to voice-of-customer research: VoC tells you what the reader cares about, and Dry's rules tell you how to say it so it sticks.

The deeper principle underneath both is that persuasion is mostly a side effect of clarity. The MECLABS research tradition frames the value proposition as the substance and the copy as the form, arguing that it should not be the form that persuades but the substance — which is why the work of sharpening the offer (the previous section) pays off more than any clever turn of phrase. For a PM reviewing a draft, a fast quality check is to read each sentence and ask Dry's three questions; the fluff falls out quickly.

The call to action

The call to action (CTA) is the button or link that asks the visitor to convert — and small changes to it produce some of the largest measured swings in conversion. The first rule is that copy beats decoration. Kissmetrics reports that "benefit-oriented verbs outperform obligation-oriented ones" — "Get Started" beats "Register," "Claim Your Spot" beats "Sign Up" — because the button should describe what the visitor gets, not what the brand wants them to do. Crazy Egg makes the same point: "Tell users what they get, not just what to do, such as 'Get 30% Off.'"

One of the best-documented findings in the field is the power of first-person framing. Kissmetrics cites a test in which changing "'Start Your Free Trial' to 'Start My Free Trial' increased conversions by 90%" — the button reads as the visitor talking to themselves rather than the brand barking an order. Specificity helps too; the same source notes a ContentVerve test where switching "'Order Information' to 'Get Your Free Quote' increased clicks by 38%."

Design then makes the well-worded button easy to find and tap. The dominant factor is contrast, not a magic color: a button should "clearly stand out from the background and everything else on the page," be generously sized, and have whitespace around it. But Kissmetrics is careful to keep the priorities straight — "Copy has a much larger impact on conversion than color." In Unbounce's teardown, the lender ooba won partly because its "descriptive button confirms the action they're about to take."

Social proof and trust

A stranger has no reason to believe your claims about yourself — so the page has to borrow credibility from people the visitor already trusts. That is the job of social proof: testimonials, customer logos, ratings, case studies, and usage numbers that show other people made the same decision and were glad they did. The standard placement, per the structure canon, is to seed trust signals high on the page and repeat them near each CTA, so proof is always within reach at the moment of decision.

Not all proof is equal. The practitioner consensus is that specific, credible, relatable proof beats generic praise: a named testimonial from someone the visitor recognizes or identifies with outperforms an anonymous five-star average, and recognizable company logos near the hero do more than a vague "trusted by thousands." This is why involve.me's structure guidance calls for testimonials "with names and faces" rather than disembodied quotes.

Scarcity and urgency are a related lever, but one to handle honestly. Unbounce's teardown notes that College Board's page hit a 77% conversion rate partly by signalling limited availability — "Seats are filling up fast!" — which works when it is true and backfires when it is a manufactured countdown the visitor has learned to distrust. The honest version of scarcity is simply telling people a real deadline or a real limit; the dishonest version trains your audience to ignore you.

Further reading: uxdesign.cc — Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

The psychology of persuasion

Social proof and scarcity are two entries in a larger catalog that almost everyone in conversion work draws on: the principles laid out by psychologist Robert Cialdini in Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (1984). Cialdini identified six principles — "reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, authority, liking, and scarcity" — drawn from "three undercover years" spent inside car dealerships, fundraising operations, and telemarketing firms. He later added a seventh, unity: "The more we identify ourselves with others, the more we are influenced by these others."

On a landing page these principles become concrete design choices. Reciprocity shows up as a genuinely useful free resource offered before any ask — "By offering something of value… we can create a sense of social obligation." Authority shows up as credentials, expert endorsements, and data — "sharing relevant qualifications or accomplishments." Commitment shows up as the small first step that leads to a bigger one: "getting individuals to commit to something small… can significantly increase the likelihood of them becoming customers."

It is worth naming the ethical line, because PMs own it. These principles describe how human decisions work; they can be used to help a visitor make a choice they will be happy with, or to trick them into one they will regret. The conversion community is not unanimous here — and the same techniques that lift a metric this quarter can erode trust over years if they shade into dark patterns (designs that manipulate rather than inform). The durable use of persuasion is to reduce the genuine hesitation around a good decision, not to engineer a bad one.

Further reading: uxdesign.cc — Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

Reducing friction

Adding persuasion is only half the work; the other half is removing the obstacles between the visitor and the conversion. The foundational text here is Steve Krug's Don't Make Me Think, whose first law of usability is to "make everything as self-evident and as self-explanatory as possible." The title is the whole argument: every moment a visitor has to stop and puzzle out what something means is a moment they might leave instead.

Krug's central observation is that people do not read web pages carefully — they scan. Because of that, designers should "establish clear visual hierarchies, define distinct page areas, highlight clickable elements, and eliminate visual clutter." Every needless question the page forces — "Where am I?", "What's important here?", "Is that a button?" — drains cognitive load, the mental effort a visitor has available, which is "better spent accomplishing their actual tasks."

On a landing page the biggest single source of friction is usually the form. The practical moves are well known: ask for the fewest fields you can live with, label them clearly, and remove anything that creates anxiety — the visitor's worry about what happens after they click (Will I be spammed? Is my card safe? Can I cancel?). This is the friction-and-anxiety side of the conversion balance from earlier: a brilliant value proposition can still be sunk by a ten-field form and no privacy reassurance. Reducing friction rarely makes a flashy case study, but it is often the cheapest conversion gain on the page.

Optimizing with experiments

Even experts guess wrong about what will convert, which is why serious teams test rather than argue. The core method is A/B testing — showing one version of a page (the control) to half your visitors and a changed version to the other half, then comparing which converts better. Shopify's guidance is to start every test from a hypothesis written as "Because you see [research] you expect that [change] will cause [impact]" and to change one variable at a time, because testing several at once means "you might not be able to determine which change caused the effect."

Reading the result correctly is where most teams go wrong. Statistical significance — the measure that "tells you if you can trust the observed results or not" — has a conventional threshold of 95% in conversion work, though that is "not universally required." The classic mistake is peeking: stopping a test the instant it crosses 95%. Because "stat sig fluctuates" day to day, the decision to stop "should be based on sample size" and a predetermined run time, not on a number that happened to look good this morning.

The most important reframe is that testing is a learning engine, not a slot machine. Shopify puts it well: if the hypothesis is sharp, "even a loser is a winner, because you'll gain insights you can use for future tests." And testing is downstream of research — even the most-optimized pages keep going, as the team behind FilterEasy's high-converting page admitted: "we're still constantly testing to figure out what's so great about this landing page." For a PM, the takeaway is that conversion rate is not a fact you discover once but a number you move, slowly and honestly, with evidence.

Common mistakes

The failure modes on landing pages are remarkably consistent, and most trace back to ignoring one of the principles above. The recurring ones are worth keeping as a checklist, because they are easy to spot once named and expensive to leave in place. None of these is exotic; they are the ordinary ways focus and clarity leak away.

  • Breaking the scent. A page whose headline doesn't echo the ad that drove the click squanders attention — the message-match failure Oli Gardner found in "98% of Marketers."
  • Too many exits. A full navigation menu and a dozen links turn a focused page back into a homepage; the fix is the "One page. One job." rule.
  • Feature-dumping. Listing what the product has instead of what the visitor gets — the opposite of the benefit-led, voice-of-customer approach the copywriters teach.
  • Fluffy copy. Lines that fail Harry Dry's three tests — you can't picture them, can't prove them, and a competitor could've written them — so the reader believes none of it: "you've probably written fluff."
  • Vague CTAs. A button that says "Submit" instead of describing the value, when first-person, benefit-led copy can move conversions dramatically — recall the "'Start My Free Trial' increased conversions by 90%" finding.
  • High-friction forms. Asking for too much, too soon, with no reassurance — the anxiety Krug's usability work warns against.
  • Shipping on opinion. Launching a redesign because it looks better in a meeting, with no hypothesis and no test, then never measuring whether it actually converted.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Landing page design and copywriting sits at the intersection of marketing, product, and design, and it borrows constantly from neighboring disciplines. The topics below are the natural next steps once the fundamentals here are in place. None are taught in this primer; they are pointers to where the threads lead.

  • Conversion-rate optimization (CRO) as a practice — the broader discipline of conversion research, heatmaps, session recordings, and prioritization frameworks that decide what to test, of which A/B testing is only the final step.
  • Growth marketing and the acquisition funnel — how landing pages fit into the larger journey from ad impression to retained customer, and where conversion sits among acquisition, activation, and retention.
  • Marketing measurement and attribution — how you credit a conversion back to the channel and campaign that produced it, so you know which landing pages and traffic sources actually pay off.
  • Fundamentals of marketing and positioning — the segmentation, targeting, and positioning work that decides who a page is for and what promise it can credibly make.
  • User experience (UX) research and usability testing — the methods, beyond A/B tests, for watching real people use a page and finding the friction that analytics alone can't explain.
  • Behavioral economics and ethics of persuasion — the deeper study of how people decide, and the line between helping a choice along and manipulating it with dark patterns.

References

  1. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    A landing page is "a single web page that appears in response to clicking on a search engine optimized search result, marketing promotion, marketing email or an online advertisement" — purpose-built for one marketing action, unlike a homepage's broad entry point. The two main types are reference pages (surround the visitor "with all available information about the targeted product to convince them to take action") and transactional pages, whose "purpose… is to persuade a visitor to take action by completing a transaction."

  2. strategyzer.com ·cited· (T1)

    Strategyzer's Value Proposition Canvas pairs a customer profile — customer jobs ("the functional, social, and emotional tasks customers try to accomplish"), pains ("the negative experiences, risks, and obstacles"), and gains ("the positive outcomes and benefits customers expect or desire") — against a value map of products/services, pain relievers, and gain creators. "The ultimate goal is achieving 'fit', or the point when your pain relievers and gain creators directly address the most important customer pains and gains."

  3. unbounce.com ·cited· (T1)

    Message match is "a measure of how well your landing page copy matches the phrasing of the ad or link that brought the visitor there." Strong message match lifts conversions because it reassures visitors they've arrived at the right place; misaligned messaging "essentially wastes the attention and trust visitors initially granted through their click."

  4. copyhackers.com ·cited· (T1)

    Joanna Wiebe of Copyhackers argues there is no universal word count: "You'll need to provide sufficient copy to tap into the visitors' motivation for coming, and amplify that motivation so they take your desired action. If you provide too little copy, you may not adequately answer the visitors' questions; too much and you risk turning them off." She prizes a high "word-to-meaning ratio" — few words carrying a ton of value.

  5. copyhackers.com ·cited· (T1)

    Voice-of-customer (VoC) copywriting taps "directly into what your customer's thinking and feeling" by using the customer's own words rather than invented claims; Joanna Wiebe notes a Sweatblock page "beat the control" because "all of the problems that follow, came directly from reviews for Sweatblock on Amazon." Review mining can be sped up with a search like `site:amazon.com inurl:"product-reviews" "tired of" [keyword]` to surface genuine pain-point language fast.

  6. growthexperts.co ·cited· (T1)

    Harry Dry of Marketing Examples gives copy three tests: visualizability ("Can I visualize it?"), falsifiability ("Can I prove it true or false?" — e.g. "get your clients from 'signup' to 'first payment' in under 24 hours"), and uniqueness ("Can nobody else write this?" — generic lines like "We deliver innovative solutions tailored for your needs" fail). "If you can't say yes to all three, you've probably written fluff."

  7. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T1)

    Robert Cialdini's *Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion* (1984) identified six principles — reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, authority, liking, and scarcity — drawn from "three undercover years" inside car dealerships, fundraising, and telemarketing firms. He added a seventh, unity, in 2016: "The more we identify ourselves with others, the more we are influenced by these others."

  8. liquidlight.co.uk ·cited· (T1)

    Steve Krug's *Don't Make Me Think* sets a first law of usability — "make everything as self-evident and as self-explanatory as possible" — and observes that users scan rather than read, so designers should "establish clear visual hierarchies, define distinct page areas, highlight clickable elements, and eliminate visual clutter." Every needless question ("Where am I?", "What's important here?") wastes cognitive resources "better spent accomplishing their actual tasks."

  9. involve.me ·cited· (T2)

    An effective landing page follows a focused sequence — Hero → Unique Value Proposition → Benefits & Features → Social Proof → Primary CTA/Form → Objection Handlers → Footer — because "the ideal landing page structure is a focused sequence that takes visitors from promise to proof to action." The governing rule is "One page. One job." — and "removing navigation menus alone can double conversion rates by reducing distractions."

  10. marketingexperiments.com ·cited· (T2)

    MECLABS' Conversion Sequence Heuristic, distilled from testing "more than 10,000 sales paths over the past 20 years," is "a thinking tool anyone can use" — "a way of looking at a sales path and going through elements of the customer's experience of a particular page or process." Founder Flint McGlaughlin frames the probability of conversion as driven by visitor motivation and the force of the value proposition, minus the friction and anxiety in the process (offset by any incentive).

  11. unbounce.com ·cited· (T2)

    Unbounce co-founder Oli Gardner examined 300 paid ads and found the vast majority failed to match their landing pages — hence "98% of Marketers are Doing it WRONG!" He defines attention ratio as "the ratio of interaction points (links) to the number of calls to action on any given page": a homepage runs around 40:1 (39 distractions to one desired action), while an optimized landing page aims for a focused 1:1.

  12. crazyegg.com ·cited· (T2)

    Crazy Egg's CTA guidance: write value-focused copy that tells users what they get ("Get 30% Off"), favor first-person variants ("Start My Free Trial"), and use action verbs over vague labels like "Submit." Make the button "clearly stand out from the background and everything else on the page," size it generously, give it whitespace, and "ensure that users can see a CTA button as soon as they arrive on the page, without having to scroll."

  13. kissmetrics.io ·cited· (T2)

    Kissmetrics reports that "benefit-oriented verbs outperform obligation-oriented ones" ('Get Started' beats 'Register'), that changing "Start Your Free Trial" to "Start My Free Trial" "increased conversions by 90%," and that a single CTA word change can swing conversions by 10–30% (a ContentVerve test lifted clicks 38% by switching "Order Information" to "Get Your Free Quote"). On color it notes contrast matters more than hue — "Copy has a much larger impact on conversion than color."

  14. netreputation.com ·cited· (T2)

    Applied to marketing, Cialdini's principles become tactics: reciprocity ("By offering something of value… we can create a sense of social obligation"), scarcity ("creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity by emphasizing the limited availability"), authority ("sharing relevant qualifications or accomplishments"), consistency ("getting individuals to commit to something small… can significantly increase the likelihood of them becoming customers"), liking ("highlighting shared experiences or beliefs"), and social proof ("highlighting how many people have already taken a certain action").

  15. convert.com ·cited· (T2)

    In A/B testing, "statistical significance tells you if you can trust the observed results or not" — whether a difference came from your change or random chance. 95% is the conventional CRO threshold but "not universally required." Crucially, significance is not a stop signal: "stat sig fluctuates" daily, so stopping the moment a test crosses 95% ("peeking") is a mistake — pausing "should be based on sample size."

  16. shopify.com ·cited· (T2)

    Shopify's A/B testing process: start from a hypothesis ("Because you see [research] you expect that [change] will cause [impact]"), change one variable at a time ("you might not be able to determine which change caused the effect" otherwise), split traffic against a control, and meet both sample-size and run-time targets before deciding. Even losing tests pay off: "even a loser is a winner, because you'll gain insights you can use for future tests."

  17. unbounce.com ·cited· (T2)

    Unbounce's teardown of high-converting pages shows the principles in the wild: edX won because "shorter copy worked better than longer copy"; Later kept "conversion scent throughout the campaign. The offer matches what's in the ad, in the email"; ooba used a "descriptive button [that] confirms the action they're about to take"; College Board (77% conversion) leaned on scarcity — "Seats are filling up fast!" — while FilterEasy's team admitted "we're still constantly testing."

  18. news.aakashg.com ·cited· (T3)

    Aakash Gupta's newsletter article on optimising how you consume and process information.

  19. writingcooperative.com ·cited· (T3)

    Breakdown of the Amazon 6-pager narrative document format, explaining its structure, purpose, and why Amazon uses long-form memos instead of slide decks to drive strategic decision-making.

  20. uxdesign.cc ·cited· (T3)

    Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

  21. uxdesign.cc ·cited· (T3)

    Analysis of Google's "messy middle" consumer decision-making model for product managers, explaining how customers oscillate between exploration and evaluation before purchase and how products can influence that journey.

  22. amazon.com ·cited· (T4)

    Amazon listing for April Dunford's book 'Obviously Awesome,' covering product positioning strategy; no content available beyond the Amazon storefront.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_landing-page-design-and-copywriting.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
